summary:
The Battle of Thermopylae (480 BC) was a three-day rearguard action fought at a narrow coastal pass in central Greece between a small Greek alliance and the vast invading army of Xerxes I of Persia, part of the [[event:1007|Xerxes I Invasion of Greece]]. The pass at Thermopylae — the 'Hot Gates' — was one of the few points where a small force could bottle up a much larger army. The Greek commander, the Spartan King Leonidas I, led approximately 7,000 troops drawn from various city-states, including his famous 300 Spartans.

For two days, the Greeks successfully repelled every Persian assault. The Persian Immortals, Xerxes's elite troops, failed to break through the compressed fighting space. On the third day, a local Greek named Ephialtes revealed a mountain path allowing the Persians to outflank the position. Leonidas sent most of the Greek forces to safety and remained with a rearguard of 300 Spartans, around 700 Thespians, and 400 Thebans to cover the retreat. All of them died fighting.

The tactical defeat was transformed into a moral victory of enormous cultural power. The Spartans died to the last man, fulfilling their tradition of never retreating in battle. Their sacrifice allowed the main Greek fleet to withdraw from [[event:1008|Battles of Artemisium]] and regroup for the decisive [[event:1005|Battle of Salamis]]. The brief epitaph inscribed at the site — "Go tell the Spartans, passerby, that here, obeying their commands, we lie" — became one of antiquity's most famous lines.

Thermopylae's legacy has been immense and complex. It has been invoked in countless contexts as the archetype of heroic self-sacrifice against overwhelming odds. In ancient Athens, it served as a foil to Marathon — where free men won by cunning tactics — and Salamis — where they won by naval strategy. The battle has been mythologized in modern popular culture, though historians note that it was a Spartan defeat, and that Sparta's motivations were as much strategic as idealistic.
